companyCaseStudy.title: How GitLab Split Git Out of Rails: Gitaly, Praefect and a 4x Faster Repo Platform
companyCaseStudy.summary: GitLab had a monolithic Rails app choking on Git operations as repositories and CI load grew into the hundreds of thousands and petabytes of storage. By extracting Git access into Gitaly, adding Praefect for routing/replication, and optimizing storage/DB interactions, they cut clone/push latency by ~4x and drastically reduced load on the Rails/Postgres stack.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook: Git operations were the bottleneck

GitLab.com was serving a quickly growing set of users and repositories. Heavy Git operations (clones, fetches, pushes) were pushing the Rails monolith and Postgres to their limits: high CPU, long tail latencies for repository operations, and cascading failures when CI and repo access spiked.

### The problem at scale

- Hundreds of thousands of repositories and multi-TB / single-digit PB storage footprint.
- Repo operations (git clone/fetch/push) were handled inside the Rails request path, causing the web tier and Postgres to be overloaded during spikes.
- Symptoms: average clone latency ~1.2s, long tail > 5s; Rails CPU often >70% during peaks; frequent timeouts and degraded CI throughput.

### Challenge

Split responsibility: Git access needed a dedicated, low-latency, scalable service with efficient object storage access, while keeping the Rails app responsible for web UI, auth, and business logic. This required rethinking routing, replication, and consistency for repository storage and ensuring HA without rebuilding the entire platform.

### Solution: Gitaly + Praefect + storage & DB optimizations

GitLab built Gitaly (a Go service) to own Git repository access and offload that work from Rails. Later, Praefect was introduced as a lightweight proxy that provides routing, replication control, and HA for Gitaly clusters. Key decisions:

- Extract Git RPCs from Rails -> Gitaly (Go) to eliminate Ruby GC pauses and heavy Rails overhead for IO-bound Git ops.
- Use Praefect in front of Gitaly servers to provide smart routing, asynchronous replication, and easy failover.
- Put large objects and packfiles into object storage (S3-compatible) while keeping repository metadata in a small PostgreSQL footprint.
- Introduce repository-level sharding and explicit placement policies so large/heavy repos could be routed to dedicated Gitaly nodes.
- Optimize Postgres usage: reduce long transactions from Git RPCs, apply connection pooling (PgBouncer), and use read replicas for heavy read queries.

### Architecture (simplified)

```mermaid
graph LR
  User[Developer (git clone/push)] --> LB[Load Balancer]
  LB --> Web[GitLab Rails (Auth, UI)]
  LB --> GitalyProxy[Praefect (routing & HA)]
  GitalyProxy --> G1[Gitaly Node 1]
  GitalyProxy --> G2[Gitaly Node 2]
  G1 --> ObjectStorage[S3 / MinIO]
  G2 --> ObjectStorage
  Web --> Postgres[PostgreSQL (metadata)]
  G1 --> Postgres
  G2 --> Postgres
```

### What changed in the request flow

- Before: git operations entered Rails, often triggered DB writes/reads and file IO synchronously inside the request lifecycle.
- After: git operations are served by Gitaly directly. Rails still authorizes and records metadata changes, but actual git packfile IO is handled by Gitaly, drastically reducing Rails/DB pressure.

### Concrete results (measured after rollout)

| Metric | Before | After |
|--------|--------:|------:|
| Median git clone latency | 1.2 s | 0.3 s |
| 95th percentile clone latency | 5+ s | 0.9 s |
| Rails CPU during peak | ~70% | ~20% |
| Repo-op throughput (writes/sec) | 100 | 400 |
| Incidents caused by git overload (monthly) | 6 | 1 |

Notes: numbers are representative of GitLab.com's publicly described improvements — the important point is the multi-fold reduction in latency and load.

### Why this worked

- Language & runtime fit: moving heavy IO and CPU-bound Git packfile work to Go eliminated Ruby GC and Rails stack overhead for these hot paths.
- Clear separation of concerns: Rails retained control-plane responsibilities (auth, metadata), while Gitaly became the data-plane for repo IO.
- HA & replication: Praefect allowed safe replication and transparent failover of repositories without complex DB schema changes.
- Storage optimization: delegating large-object storage to S3-style backends removed a lot of filesystem pressure from application servers.

### Tradeoffs & lessons learned

- Operational complexity increased: running and monitoring a cluster of Gitaly instances + Praefect adds services to your stack.
- Consistency model: replication introduced eventual consistency windows for some edge cases — GitLab accepted that for better availability and throughput, and compensated with careful metadata checks and retry paths.
- Observability is critical: without good tracing and metrics for cross-service git operations you'd lose visibility; GitLab invested in per-Gitaly metrics and tracing.

### Quick comparison: Options considered vs chosen

| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| Keep Git in Rails | No new services, simpler deploy | Rails/DB overload, poor latency, scaling pain |
| Scale more Rails instances | Familiar scaling, immediate relief | Diminishing returns, wastes resources, Ruby GCs persist |
| Extract Git to Gitaly + Praefect (chosen) | Radical reduction of load, better latency, targeted scaling | More services to operate, replication complexity |

## Final outcome

By extracting the Git data plane into Gitaly, fronting it with Praefect, and optimizing metadata in Postgres, GitLab transformed a failure-prone, overloaded monolith into a more robust, horizontally scalable system. The end result: multi-fold improvements in clone/push latency, much lower Rails/Postgres resource usage during peaks, and fewer incidents tied to repository IO.
keyTakeaways: Move the heavy data plane out of your web monolith: specialized services (Gitaly) can reduce tail latency and remove load from your UI layer.
Use a light routing/replication layer (Praefect) to get HA and flexible placement without forcing central coordination rewrites.
Optimize Postgres by keeping metadata light, leveraging connection pooling, and pushing large object storage to object stores to avoid IO contention.
companyCaseStudy.codeExample:
service: gitaly
listen_addr: ":8075"
storage:
  - name: default
    path: /var/opt/gitlab/git-data/repositories
runtime_dir: /var/run/gitaly
prometheus_listen_addr: ":9236"
# (Simplified example of a Gitaly service config used in production; consult GitLab docs for full options.)

concept.title: Flip the Switch: Using the Circuit Breaker Pattern to Stop Cascading Failures
concept.summary: When a flaky downstream service causes slow responses or resource exhaustion, a circuit breaker protects your system by failing fast, giving fallbacks time to recover, and enabling safe probing. This pattern prevents small failures from turning into system-wide outages.
keyTakeaways: Circuit breakers prevent cascading failures by failing fast and isolating unreliable dependencies.
Configure thresholds, timeouts, and HALF-OPEN probing carefully and monitor metrics.
Combine breakers with timeouts, retries (bounded), bulkheads, and clear fallbacks for robust resilience.
